title: Monoset
desc: A minimal, monotone, unopinionated design system.
body: Monoset design system. Tokens and React components. One neutral ramp. One typeface. Radix-backed components. Monotone. Minimal. Unopinionated.

title: Introduction
desc: What Monoset is, what ships in the box, and when to reach for it.
body: The approach What ships Built for AI workflows Where to start

title: Installation
desc: Install Monoset via the CSS tokens or the React component kit.
body: Three ways in, depending on how deep you're going. The starter CLI is the fastest for a new project, the React kit is the usual pick, and the tokens-only package is there if you're not using React. Scaffolds a Vite + React 19 project pre-wired with Monoset. Zero configuration. The component library plus the tokens and the Framer Motion presets. If you already have a React app, this is the path. If you're building components yourself, or your stack isn't React, pull in just the token file and style against the CSS variables. Inter for UI, JetBrains Mono for code. Both open source, both self-hosted. The easiest path is @fontsource : one CSS import per weight, files get bundled by Vite, zero third-party requests. Prefer Google Fonts instead? Drop a <link> into your HTML head and skip the npm packages. The tokens work either way. Starter CLI (new project) React kit (existing project) CSS tokens only (any framework) Fonts

title: Basic usage
desc: An end-to-end example with Monoset tokens and primitives.
body: A quick end-to-end example: a sign-in card built with Monoset tokens and primitives. Sign in Use your workspace email. Email Password Continue `} Dark theme Add the monoset-dark class to any element and the semantic tokens flip for everything inside it. No ThemeProvider, no context. Put it on <html> for a whole-app switch, or on a single panel for an inverted card. Full example Dark theme

title: Colors
desc: The 12-step neutral ramp and the semantic tokens built on top of it.
body: One 12-step neutral ramp. Backgrounds, text, borders, and shadows all pull from it. No hues and no gradients. The constraint is the whole point. Reach for the semantic tokens ( --bg , --fg1 , --border ) inside components. Raw ramp values ( --mono-500 ) are for defining new semantic tokens, not for sprinkling into UI. Otherwise the dark-mode flip misses you. Neutral ramp Semantic tokens

title: Typography
desc: Inter for UI, JetBrains Mono for code, a modular scale from 11px to 64px.
body: Inter for UI and body, JetBrains Mono for code. A scale from 11px to 64px (hit ratios, not arbitrary sizes) with two ways to create contrast: tone and size. Weight does some of the work too, but tone carries it. Type scale Families

title: Spacing & radii
desc: A 4px base grid with named spacing and radius tokens.
body: A 4px base grid with named tokens. Components snap to these values; resist the urge to sneak in a stray 7px padding because it "looks better". It usually doesn't, and the system falls apart one off-grid value at a time. Everything that isn't a pill uses --radius-md (6px). Keeping one radius across inputs, buttons, cards and menus makes surfaces feel like they came from the same kit. --radius-full is reserved for avatars and status chips. Spacing scale Radii

title: Motion
desc: Three durations, one easing curve. Monoset's approach to UI motion.
body: Three durations and one easing curve. Fast and quiet by design: no springs, no bounces, nothing that hangs around for 900ms. Motion should confirm what just happened, then get out of the way. Easing Durations

title: Framer Motion
desc: JS-driven animation presets that match the CSS motion tokens.
body: Reach for Framer Motion when CSS transitions hit their limit, like FLIP layout animations or anything that needs to interrupt itself mid-play. Same rules as the CSS tokens: short durations, one curve, no springs. A handful of presets, shared across the kit. Same easings and durations as the CSS tokens, so your JS-driven animations match the CSS-driven ones. Start here. whileHover and whileTap cover 80% of what you'll ever need. Pick one property (a 2px lift or a shade), keep it under 180ms, and don't stack them. Hover me Shade on hover Enter on mount A small fade and 8px rise on mount. Use it for first-paint reveals, not for every re-render. The animation loses meaning if it plays every time state changes. Mount / unmount Without AnimatePresence , unmounts just vanish. Wrap conditional children and they get an exit. Monoset's exit is a quick opacity drop, no slide, no scale; sliding panels off-screen is noisier than they deserve. setOpen(o => !o)} style= }> panel `} Layout transitions Share a layoutId across mounted siblings and Framer does the FLIP math. The tab indicator below slides between items without you writing a single pixel offset. style= }> ))} `} Rules , , ) for UI. It introduces bounce. Reserve springs for physical drag." }, , ].map((r,i) => ( ))} Reduced motion One wrap at the app root and the whole system respects the OS preference. People who opted out get opacity-only transitions; no 8px rises, no slides. Install Monoset motion presets Hover & press Enter on mount Mount / unmount Layout transitions Rules Reduced motion

title: Button
desc: Four variants, three sizes, four states. A React Button component.
body: Four variants across three sizes. Keep labels short (two or three words) and write them in sentence case. All-caps looks shouty in a monotone system. Variants Sizes States

title: Input
desc: Text fields, textareas, and selects with a Field wrapper.
body: Text fields, textareas, selects. Wrap them in a Field and you get the label, helper text, and error state wired up. Otherwise you'll end up copy-pasting the same aria boilerplate around every input. Field States

title: Badge
desc: Small labels for status, counts, and category tags.
body: Small labels for status, counts, and category tags. Three variants, all monotone, because a badge that screams louder than the thing it's on defeats the point. Neutral Outline Solid Status with dot Add a colored dot for statuses that need to be scannable at a glance. Useful in tables and list views. ))} Tags Dismissible tags for filters, categories, or multi-select inputs. × ))} In context Badges sit next to nav labels for unread counts, inside table cells for row status, or beside headings for version numbers. Variants Status with dot Tags In context

title: Card
desc: Surface containers: outline, elevated, and inset variants.
body: A surface container. Outline by default. Use the elevated variant when a card needs to feel lifted off the page, or the inset variant inside forms and modals where the surrounding panel already does the grouping. Outline Elevated Inset

title: Checkbox & Switch
desc: Two similar controls with different semantics.
body: Two similar-looking controls with different semantics. Use a checkbox when the choice is part of a form that gets submitted. Use a switch when flipping it takes effect right away, like email notifications or dark mode. Checkbox Switch

title: Table
desc: Dense rows with hairline separators and subtle hover.
body: Dense rows with hairline separators. Pass `maxHeight` and the wrapper caps its height with the header pinned. Narrower viewports scroll sideways. Pass maxHeight and the wrapper caps its height and scrolls. The thead stays pinned. Example Scrollable with sticky header

title: Tabs
desc: Underline tabs for pages, segmented tabs for inline toggles.
body: Underline tabs for page-level navigation like Account, Billing, Security. Segmented tabs for inline switches inside a panel. Don't mix the two on one page; they start competing for attention. style= }> ))} Segmented The pill style for inline switches inside a panel. Time ranges, view modes, filter toggles. style= }> ))} Controlled For controlled usage, pass value and onValueChange instead of defaultValue . Tabs wraps Radix UI Tabs. All Radix props are forwarded. Underline tabs Segmented Controlled

title: Alert & Toast
desc: Inline alerts and transient toasts, both monotone.
body: Inline alerts sit in the layout and stay there until the problem is fixed. Toasts pop in, say their piece, and disappear. Both stay monotone. No red-green traffic light; the icon and the copy do the work. Inline alert Toast Usage

title: Avatar
desc: Initials on a neutral background. Three sizes, stackable.
body: Initials on a neutral background. Three sizes. Pass an image URL and it renders the photo; leave it blank and you get the initials fallback. They stack, and when the group gets crowded an overflow chip shows how many more. Three named sizes. sm for dense lists and table rows, md for cards and nav, lg for profiles and headers. ))} Image with initials fallback When src is set, the image renders. If it fails to load (broken URL, 404), the initials show instead. No layout shift either way. AT GH LB `} Stacked group Overlap avatars with a negative margin. The overflow chip at the end shows the remaining count. ))} }/> ))} `} In a list A common pattern: avatar next to a name and subtitle in a compact row. Sizes Image with initials fallback Stacked group In a list

title: Accordion
desc: Disclosure panels. Keyboard and screen reader behavior from Radix.
body: A disclosure pattern. The headings stay visible, the bodies collapse by default. Keyboard and screen reader behavior comes from Radix, so you don't have to wire any of it yourself. , , , ].map(item => ( ))} API Props forward to @radix-ui/react-accordion . Set type="single" so only one panel opens at a time, or type="multiple" if you want people to open several. Default API

title: Slider
desc: A range input for continuous values like volume or opacity.
body: A range input. Good for values that feel continuous, like volume or opacity. The thumb is small on purpose; put the current value next to the label so people don't have to squint at it. Volume setSlider(+e.target.value)} style= }/> API Wraps @radix-ui/react-slider . Pass an array of two numbers if you want a min/max range with two thumbs. Default API

title: Toggle group
desc: A segmented control for mutually-exclusive options.
body: A segmented control. Use it when the options are mutually exclusive and you can fit them on one line. Past four options it gets cramped; a Select does that job better. , , , ].map(opt => ( setView(opt.v)} style= }> ))} Single toggle The standalone Toggle is for a single on/off action, like a bold button in a toolbar or a pin on a list item. style= }> ))} With icons Toggle groups work well for toolbar-style controls. Pass icon elements as children for compact controls like alignment or view switchers. Default Single toggle With icons

title: Kbd
desc: Keyboard shortcut chips for menus and tooltips.
body: Renders a keyboard shortcut. The little ⌘K chip you see in search bars and menus. Use it anywhere a user might reach for the mouse when they didn't have to. The chips below light up when you press the matching key. ⌘K (or Ctrl+K ) opens the fake command palette. ))} : "Press something…"} In context Open command bar ⌘ K Sizes sm for inside body text, md (the default) for menus and tooltips. Try it: press a key In context Sizes

Spinner
desc: A monotone loading indicator for waits over 400ms.
body: For waits longer than ~400ms when you don't have a better placeholder. Anything faster, skip it; a spinner that flashes for 80ms just looks broken. Pass a pixel value. Match the spinner to the text size around it. px ))} Inside a button Put it in a button while the request is in flight. Leave the label in place; swapping "Save" for the spinner makes the button width jump and the eye catches it. `} Inline with text Works in any inline context. Match the size to the surrounding font. Sizes Inside a button Inline with text

title: Textarea
desc: Multi-line text input with Field wrapper support.
body: A multi-line text input. Same styling as Input, same Field wrapper pattern. Use it for comments, descriptions, notes, and anything that might run past a single line. Description Supports markdown formatting. With Field wrapper Wrap in Field for label, helper text, and error handling. Same pattern as Input. Props Textarea accepts all native <textarea> attributes plus: Default With Field wrapper States

title: Select
desc: A dropdown for picking one option from a list. Wraps Radix UI Select.
body: A dropdown menu for picking one option from a list. Wraps Radix UI Select for keyboard navigation, screen reader support, and portal rendering. Use it when you have five or more options; under five, a Toggle group is usually cleaner. Role Admin Member Viewer Billing With placeholder The placeholder prop on SelectTrigger shows when no value is selected. Selects work inside Field wrappers and with useMonosetForm just like other inputs. Default With placeholder In a form

title: Layout
desc: Stack, Inline, Grid, and Container primitives for spacing and structure.
body: Four layout primitives that handle spacing so you never write display: flex; gap: 16px by hand again. Stack for vertical, Inline for horizontal, Grid for auto-fit columns, Container for max-width centering. Vertical spacing. The gap prop maps to the --space-* token scale (0-14). ))} Inline Horizontal with wrap. Good for tag lists, button rows, or any set of items that should flow left to right and wrap naturally. ))} Grid Auto-fit responsive grid. Cards wrap to the next row when they can't fit at minWidth . No breakpoints needed. ))} Container Centers content with a max-width. Five sizes from sm (640px) to 2xl (1536px). Adds horizontal padding by default. These primitives are designed to nest. A typical page layout stacks sections vertically, each section uses a Container, and the content inside uses Grid or Inline. Stack Inline Grid Container Composing layouts

title: LLM naming prompt
desc: A prompt for adding stable data-ms attributes across a React codebase.
body: Paste the prompt below into any coding agent to add stable data-ms names to your app. With stable names in place, your Playwright tests, your analytics selectors, and your next design-tool integration stop breaking every time someone renames a className. Class names get renamed when someone reaches for a new CSS framework. Layouts get rewritten when a redesign lands. A data-ms named by what the thing is for , rather than how it looks, tends to survive both. Monoset uses this pattern internally: the landing page carries data-ms="bento-player" and data-ms="hero-title" , which is why the responsive CSS can target them without caring about the markup underneath. Run it on one page or feature at a time. Whole-app passes tend to produce generic names that all sound the same. Once they're in place, the rest is boring. Any tool that speaks CSS selectors can find what it needs: Class names are styling concerns. They change when you refactor CSS, swap to Tailwind, or rename a component. IDs are unique but brittle and overloaded (anchor links, ARIA, form controls). Data attributes are the only selector that carries no styling or behavioral meaning, which is exactly what makes them stable across rewrites. Three things benefit from stable selectors: Different UI patterns need different naming strategies. Here's what works for common Monoset patterns: A complete Playwright test that uses data-ms selectors. No fragile class names, no text matchers that break with copy changes. When your codebase has data-ms attributes, AI tools like Cursor, Copilot, and Claude Code can reference UI regions by name instead of guessing at the DOM structure. This makes prompts like "add a loading state to the users-table" unambiguous. Add a note to your project's AI instructions (CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, etc.): Why semantic names The prompt Naming conventions Using the names Why design systems need stable selectors Component-specific conventions Playwright test example Using with AI coding tools

title: Theming Monoset
desc: Dark mode, accent colors, and scoped themes.
body: Monoset ships light and dark modes, scoped theme overrides, and a single accent channel you can swap in one line of CSS. Monoset flips the entire neutral ramp when the root element carries data-monoset-theme="dark" or the .monoset-dark class. Every semantic token (--bg, --fg1, --border, etc.) re-maps automatically. No extra imports, no separate stylesheet. Wrap your app in MonosetProvider and pass your default theme. The provider reads the user's system preference on first load and syncs it to localStorage. Read and set the current theme from anywhere inside the provider tree. Drop in the built-in toggle for a sun/moon switch that works out of the box. You can force dark mode on a single container without touching the rest of the page. Add the .monoset-dark class to any element, and every Monoset token inside it flips. Monoset uses three CSS custom properties for the accent color channel: --accent , --accent-fg , and --accent-hover . Override them at any scope to swap the brand color without rebuilding anything. Every Monoset component that uses accent (primary buttons, focused inputs, active tabs) picks up the new values automatically. How dark mode works Scoped dark mode Customizing the accent

Building a settings page
desc: A real-world pattern composing Tabs, Cards, and form controls.
body: A settings page is a good stress test for any component library. It needs tabs, form controls, cards, validation, and a layout that holds up on mobile. Here is how the pieces fit together in Monoset. Tabs across the top, cards for each section, and a save bar pinned to the bottom. This is the pattern most teams end up with. The useMonosetForm hook wires validation to Field's error state. Pass a map of field names to validation functions and the hook returns helpers for the rest. Container caps the width and centers the content. Stack handles vertical spacing between form fields. Both are thin wrappers that apply Monoset spacing tokens so you don't end up with magic numbers in every file. Full settings layout Form validation Layout with Stack and Container

Accessibility checklist
desc: What Monoset handles, what you handle, and how to test.
body: Monoset components are built on Radix UI and ship accessible by default. Keyboard support, focus management, and ARIA attributes are handled for you. This page lists what you get and what you still need to add. Use vitest-axe to catch regressions in your own pages. The test below checks that a rendered settings page has no accessibility violations. Monoset handles component-level accessibility. Page-level concerns are still on you: What Monoset handles Testing with axe What you need to add

title: Accessibility checklist · What you need to add
Using with Next.js
desc: App Router, Pages Router, and Server Component setup.
body: Monoset works with both the App Router and Pages Router. The setup takes two minutes. Remix gets its own section at the bottom. Import the CSS file in your root layout and wrap the children with MonosetProvider . The provider needs "use client" because it manages theme state with React context. ); } // Extracted so the "use client" boundary stays narrow "use client"; function Providers( : ) ); }`} In practice you would put the Providers component in its own file ( app/providers.tsx ) with the "use client" directive at the top. The example above keeps it inline for clarity. For the Pages Router the setup goes in _app.tsx . No client boundary needed since everything in Pages Router is client-rendered by default. Most Monoset components use Radix UI primitives under the hood, which means they run client-side JavaScript. Any component that handles user interaction (Button, Switch, Tabs, Accordion, Slider, etc.) needs to be inside a "use client" boundary. Layout primitives like Container , Stack , and Card are plain HTML wrappers with no interactivity. You can render them in Server Components without a client boundary. Remix uses a similar pattern. Import the CSS in your root route and wrap the Outlet with the provider. Installation App Router setup Pages Router setup Server Components With Remix

MCP server
desc: Give your AI coding agent direct access to Monoset components, tokens, and docs.
body: Give your AI coding agent direct access to Monoset's components, tokens, and documentation. No browser tabs, no hallucinated props. The agent knows what's available and writes correct code the first time. MCP (Model Context Protocol) is a standard way for AI tools to talk to external systems. Monoset's MCP server exposes five tools your agent can call: Claude Code picks up MCP servers from your project settings or global config. Add Monoset to your project: That's it. On your next conversation, Claude Code can query Monoset directly. Try asking it to "add a sortable user table with status badges" and watch it pull the right components. Add this to your Claude Desktop config file: Restart Claude Desktop. The Monoset tools appear in the tools panel. Both editors support MCP via the same stdio protocol. Add a .cursor/mcp.json to your project root: When your agent has the MCP server connected, prompts like these just work: The agent calls list_components to see what's available, get_component to get the exact props and import paths, and search_docs to find relevant guides. No guessing, no hallucination. Most design systems expect you to read their docs in a browser and translate what you see into code. That workflow breaks the moment you're using an AI coding agent, because the agent can't see your browser. It guesses at prop names, invents components that don't exist, and uses APIs from the wrong version. The MCP server closes that gap. Your agent has the same knowledge a human developer gets from the docs site, but structured as data it can act on immediately. The result: fewer corrections, fewer hallucinations, faster builds. The MCP server pairs well with data-ms attributes from the LLM naming guide. When your components carry stable semantic names and your agent understands the full component API, you get a workflow where the AI can both build new UI and modify existing UI by targeting named regions. The selectors survive redesigns, and the agent always knows what

Building a dashboard
desc: Sidebar, stat cards, and a data table in one layout.
body: A sidebar, a header, stat cards, and a data table. This is the layout you end up building for every SaaS admin panel. Here's how it comes together with Monoset. The outer frame is a CSS grid: a fixed sidebar on the left, the main content area on the right. The header sits inside the content area, not spanning the full width. A vertical nav with grouped links. The active item gets a muted background. A responsive grid of summary metrics. The Grid component handles the responsive layout; each card is just a Card with a title, value, and optional trend indicator. Below the stat cards, a sortable table with row selection. Use TableHeader for sortable columns and TableSelectAll / TableSelectRow for the checkboxes. The full dashboard page composes all three sections inside the shell. The shell Sidebar Stat cards Data table Putting it together

title: Building a dashboard · Putting it together
Data table with filters
desc: Searchable, sortable, filterable table with row selection and bulk actions.
body: A searchable, filterable, sortable data table with row selection and bulk actions. The pattern you need every time someone says "can we add an admin view for this?" A table of users with: a search input that filters by name or email, a select dropdown that filters by role, sortable column headers, row checkboxes, and a bulk action bar that appears when rows are selected. An Input above the table. Filter rows on each keystroke. A Select for role filtering. Combine it with the search filter. Use TableHeader with an onSort callback. Keep the sort state in a single object. Track selected row IDs in a Set. Show the bulk action bar only when the set is non-empty. Everything wired together. What we're building Search filter Column filter Sort Row selection and bulk actions Full example
